text: The surgeon completes the right retro-rectus dissection, with ports inserted in the left lateral retro-rectus space, medial to the left linea semilunaris. The right posterior rectus sheath incision is completed, and the right retro-rectus space is being developed in preparation for the transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon dissects below the arcuate line, where the posterior rectus sheath visibly attenuates. The dissection is completed in this area, demonstrating the thinning of the posterior rectus sheath layers.

The surgeon performs a dissection below the arcuate line, identifying multiple tissue layers beyond the peritoneum. The dissection progresses into the prepregnal space, where the prepregnal fat is easily separated from the lateral abdominal wall. The inguinal portion of the dissection is briefly addressed, revealing a second arcuate line formed by attenuated posterior rectus tissue.

The surgeon enters the desired preperitoneal space, where the preperitoneal fat is easily separated from the lateral abdominal wall, revealing the transverse abdominis muscle. The surgeon sweeps the peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath to facilitate the TAR procedure, highlighting the linea semilunaris during the process.

The surgeon sweeps preperitoneal fat off the transverse abdominis, revealing the linea semilunaris. The fat is easily removed, though small vessels may require cautery. The dissection plane is clear, but the surgeon avoids moving too lateral into the groin area, continuing dissection near the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon approaches the seam of the linea alba, where the anterior and posterior rectus sheaths converge, ensuring precise alignment during the first stage of ETEP. The focus is on identifying the junction where the posterior lamella, anterior lamella, and anterior oblique intersect or diverge.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundles as a landmark indicating proximity to the linea semilinare. Hemostasis is achieved to stop any bleeding before continuing with the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the posterior rectus sheath, entering the plane between the preperitoneal fat and the posterior rectus sheath. The fat is swept off, revealing the peritoneum, allowing for quick division of the posterior rectus sheath. The seam of the linea semilunaris is visible, approximately one centimeter away, ensuring a safe distance during the procedure.

The surgeon adjusts the path for dividing the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to injure the linea semilunaris, which is visible and not medializing much in this patient. The posterior rectus sheath consists of the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis aponeurosis. The retrorectus space is fully dissected, facilitating the division of the posterior rectus sheath in the lower half.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the posterior rectus sheath to avoid perforating the peritoneum during division. The dissection progresses laterally, entering a plane closer to the peritoneum, which is relevant for inguinal hernia repairs.

The surgeon leaves the fat up against the transverse abdominis, getting closer to the peritoneum. As the dissection transitions into the lower abdomen and pelvis, the surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum, aiming to be right on the testicular vessels and vas deferens.

The surgeon observes the peritoneum not separating well from the transversalis fascia and transitions into the space between the transversalis fascia and the transverse abdominis muscle. On the right side, the transversalis fascia is visibly lifting off the transverse abdominis muscle, indicating entry into the pre-transversalis fascia plane. This plane is preferred for dissection in the upper abdomen, staying close to the transverse abdominis muscle up to the costal margin.

The surgeon maintains dissection against the diaphragm muscle, continuing in the same plane under the ribs. The approach involves a bottoms-up TAR technique, transitioning to a top-down TAR when the area becomes challenging. The dissection remains between the transverse salus fascia and the transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon dissects within the pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen, carefully moving bowel out of the way to safely perform the TAR. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is cut first, followed by the muscular transversus abdominis, while ensuring not to disrupt the diaphragm contribution medially.

The surgeon navigates behind the xiphoid process, carefully avoiding the vessel that typically bleeds at the edges. The diaphragm is identified, ensuring it is not positioned below the surgical field. The surgeon opens the space adjacent to the diaphragm, revealing its muscle fibers in a cotton candy-like appearance.

The surgeon scores the path along the thick transverse abdominus muscle, using scissors to lift the muscle edge and applying cautery to divide it. The top-down TAR is connected to the bottom-up TAR, with spot coagulation applied to control bleeding from the muscle.

The surgeon is positioned under the diaphragm on the right side and is working to develop the space on the left side. The goal is to stay close to the diaphragm muscle and transverse abdominis muscle. The surgeon divides the bridging fascia, which appears fatty, to combine the two spaces laterally, ensuring to remain in the pre-transverse salus plane against the muscle.

The surgeon completes the division of the transversus abdominis muscle and proceeds with lateral retromuscular dissection. The surgeon carefully pulls the colon away to facilitate the TAR procedure, navigating interdigitating fibers from the transverse salus fascia between the muscle. This maneuver aims to avoid excessive cautery near the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully cauterizes along the edge of the transverse abdominis muscle, avoiding the transversalis fascia, to maintain hemostasis and expedite the dissection. As separation between the muscle and fascia increases, the cauterization is performed between these structures.

The surgeon cauterizes along the edge of the muscle, progressing with dissection until the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath are positioned over the bowel. Laterally, the dissection enters a different anatomical space.

The surgeon navigates between two fat layers, opting to stay closer to the peritoneum. As they move laterally, they continue to divide the posterior layer under slight tension. The left hand provides retraction, creating a 'cotton candy' appearance in the tissue, while the surgeon maintains the dissection on the peritoneum side.

The surgeon provides an overview of the surgical field after completing the transversus abdominis release (TAR). The posterior rectus sheath is shown reaching the midline, indicating completion. In the lower part, further dissection is performed, resembling a TAP dissection, with the peritoneum being peeled off the posterior structures. Effective left-hand retraction creates a 'cotton candy' appearance, guiding the dissection plane.

The surgeon elevates the tissue towards the midline, creating a cotton candy-like appearance, revealing the obturator space. A visible valley indicates the plane for dissection, with the left hand providing exposure to guide the path of dissection.

The surgeon prepares to place three new ports and redock to initiate the transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side. The left retroactive space has already been dissected and opened, allowing for further surgical progress.

The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, cutting the attenuated posterior rectus sheath in the left pelvis area. The surgeon's hand grasps the peritoneum while identifying the plane where preperitoneal fat separates from the transversus abdominis. The linea semilunaris is visible.

The surgeon demonstrates the linea semilunaris during the inguinal dissection, closely following the peritoneum. The pubis is visible, and the left hand elevates the tissue for better visualization. A remnant of the round ligament is observed.

The surgeon uses the left hand for retraction to expose the tissue plane, allowing the use of scissors as a hot blade to score and open the planes. The visible tissue plane reveals the area where the fat separates from the transverse abdominis, creating a large cavity.

The surgeon sweeps the preperitoneal fat and peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to injure the linea semilunaris. The posterior rectus sheath is then divided, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon demonstrates the ease of separating the preperitoneal fat from the underlying structures, emphasizing the importance of inspecting beneath the posterior rectus sheath to identify and cauterize small vessels to prevent bleeding in the lateral space. The peritoneum is carefully swept off the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris and uses cautery to divide small vessels. A new port is placed medial to the linea semilunaris for the ETEP approach.

The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection and division of the posterior rectus sheath, adjusting lighting to improve visibility. The peritoneum appears adhered to the transversalis fascia, prompting entry into the pre-transversalis fascia plane. The surgeon navigates this plane, noting the difficulty in separating the peritoneum from the fascia.

The surgeon carefully pushes the transversalis fascia off, which is more challenging than pushing the peritoneum. They stay medial to a visible neurovascular bundle. As the peritoneum becomes more difficult to separate, the surgeon gains a view behind the posterior rectus sheath and proceeds to divide it.

The surgeon evaluates the surgical field to determine the appropriate dissection plane, aiming to stay close to the muscle. Blood is visible on the tissue, complicating the process. On the right side, the correct plane has been established, and the surgeon attempts to connect it with the left side using cautery, aided by effective left-hand retraction.

The surgeon uses good left hand retraction to allow the tissue to pop when applying cautery, continuing the dissection from the bottom up. The surgeon progresses without looking cephalad, ensuring the correct path is maintained.

The surgeon begins dissection into the muscular portion of the transverse abdominis, aiming to stay close to the muscle. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is divided, with scoring used to maintain the correct surgical path.

The surgeon aggressively uses cautery on the thick transverse abdominis muscle, inserting scissors behind the muscle to facilitate the transversus abdominis release (TAR). The scissors are inserted, lifted, and cautery is applied to create a score through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique, proceeding in a bottoms-up fashion to complete the TAR on the patient's left side.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field to avoid entering the space between the diaphragm and the transversalis fascia. The exploration reveals a well-fused diaphragm to the posterior rectus sheath, facilitating the dissection. The surgeon identifies the areolar plane adjacent to the diaphragm muscle, ensuring the dissection remains close to the transverse abdominus muscle and diaphragm, joining the two spaces.

The surgeon cauterizes along the edge of the transverse abdominis muscle, completing its division. The retromuscular dissection proceeds, revealing more areolar tissue with less transverse salus fascia interdigitating between the muscle layers. The procedure is complete when the peritoneum and posterior rectus sheath rest on the bowel, and the edges of the posterior rectus sheath meet.
